data.title: AMD: Q3 Guidance Leaves Out China -- A Trade Deal Could Be A Nice Surprise
data.description:
I reiterate my Strong Buy on Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) after a resilient Q2 that shrugged off doubts about China's export control restrictions and managed to keep gross margins intact.

In my last coverage, I identified a clear trend reversal with several catalysts ahead, including the ramp of the MI350 series, the partnerships with Humain, and a massive cluster buildout at Oracle. There were also signs of insider buying activity, with COO Guido Philip buying $1 million worth of shares back in mid-May this year.

Looking ahead at Q3 earnings, the MI350 GPU ramp, paired with record EPYC CPU sales, and strong momentum in the client segment, will most likely push revenue above the Street's projected 27% YOY top-line target. Furthermore, management didn't include sales in China in their guidance, which I believe is a nice catalyst ahead, as the US will most likely close a deal soon with China.

Sovereign AI demand is ticking up, with a $10B Saudi rollout over the next 5 years and over 40 active governments in talks with AMD. Q2 brought $7.7 billion of sales and $1 billion in free cash flow, this time, led by consumer and gaming strength.

The MI400 Helios is another catalyst to take into account for 2026, which targets a tenfold performance jump compared to the previous generation. I believe this GPU will directly compete with Nvidia’s (NVDA) B300 series.

Overall, I remain hyper bullish on the company, even though there are some risks related to sectoral tariffs on semiconductors due to AMD's supplier footprint in Asia. That said, I believe only one of the four key partners is at risk of being subject to the steep, 100% tariffs on semis

Heading Into a Clear Growth Cycle

Revenue in Q2 reached $7.7 billion, despite the headwinds due to the export restrictions related to the MI308 GPU to China. As noted in the latest 10-K, AMD's revenue concentration in China was slightly over 24%.

When earnings came out, I was really impressed that the sequential decline in consolidated revenue was only 420 bps, despite the sharp drop (4,920 bps) in the data center revenue (see chart below) due to the export controls.

The consumer and gaming segments pushed the growth in both the top and bottom line in Q2. Free cash flow crossed the billion-dollar mark, mainly driven by record EPYC and Ryzen unit sales.

Furthermore, the heavier tilt toward higher margin EPYC CPUs drove the non-GAAP gross margin to 54% for the sixth straight annual increase, a figure that obviously excludes the $800 million inventory write-down tied to the U.S. export controls. Most importantly, CFO Jean Hu guided Q3 gross margins (non-GAAP) to remain at approximately 54%.

Looking at demand in the last quarter, management mentioned big OEM partners, including HPE, Dell, Lenovo, and Super Micro, that collectively rolled out 28 Turin platforms. To me, the most important one is the win with Oracle, which secured a 27,000-node AI cluster integrating MI355X accelerators, 5th-generation EPYC CPUs, and Pollara 400 NICs. This is high margin revenue, so I expect gross margins to stay in the mid-50s, at least over the next 9 months. Why?

Well, the latest Q&A session revealed an estimate for the deployment timeline of these large clusters.

So yes, I mean, our lead times are long, given all of the processing steps that we have to go through. Think about it as somewhere between 8, 9 months, that type of thing.

To support this increase in demand, AMD expects a steep back half of the year with a focus on the ramp-up of the MI350 series. The Street is already pricing in a 27% YOY increase in the topline due to this ramp-up.

Overall, I believe there are enough tailwinds to meet these high expectations. One of them is sovereign AI. It seems that large governments are taking notice of AMD. A multibillion-dollar collaboration with HUMAIN in Saudi Arabia has entered initial deployment, and CEO Lisa Su noted during the call (emphasis added) that more than 40 other sovereign engagements are currently active worldwide.

In addition, we have more than 40 active engagements globally and see significant opportunities to power an increasingly larger portion of national computing centers and sovereign AI initiatives.

Aside from the MI350 ramp, which is now competing head-to-head with Nvidia's Blackwell B200 GPU, the second most important focus is on the development of the MI400 Helios GPUs, targeting 40 petaflops of FP4 compute and a projected 10x generational leap when the series launches in 2026.

Do you remember the completion of the ZT Systems acquisition back in March this year? Well, it turns out that ZT Systems plays a key role in the rack-scale design of this new generation of GPUs. Now, the $4.9 billion that AMD spent to acquire ZT Systems makes sense to me, even though I wasn't so certain back then, as the acquisition involved integrating over 1,000 engineers. Looking at production, Sanmina is set to purchase ZT’s U.S. manufacturing arm for $3 billion by year-end, pending approvals, of course. As for me, I like this deal, as I prefer AMD to solely focus on design and leave manufacturing to its partners. Just to be clear, after the deal with Sanmina, AMD will still retain ZT Systems' AI systems design business.

Last, but not least, the imminent approval of U.S. licenses for the MI308 shipments to China is a nice catalyst ahead.

Risks

For good or for worse, tariffs on semiconductors are back in the spotlight. President Trump is now eyeing 100% tariffs on chip imports, but there is a large caveat: there is an exemption for companies that manufacture or commit to manufacturing in the U.S.

So, I decided to take a closer look at AMD's manufacturing footprint. Here is what I found in the latest 10-K filed:

The file reveals a few names, so I decided to analyze their footprint in the U.S. and whether they have any plans to invest in this country.

  • TSMC: I believe it's most likely exempt from sectoral tariffs due to its footprint in Arizona and its $165 billion commitment to invest in the U.S.
  • Tongfu Microelectronics Co., Ltd: This is a publicly listed Chinese semiconductor company headquartered in Nantong, specializing in testing and packaging. I have been unable to find any plants in the U.S. or any plans to invest in the near term, so I believe it is likely they will be subject to the tariffs.
  • Siliconware Precision Industries Ltd.: a publicly traded company founded in 1984 and based in Taichung, Taiwan. It's also focused on packaging and testing, but this company has presence in the U.S.: Siliconware USA Inc. (sales/FAE office, San Jose, CA) and ISE Labs (two engineering/test labs in Fremont & San Jose, CA) Furtermore, ASE (the parent company) said on April 30, 2025 that it is evaluating an invitation from Nvidia to build advanced-packaging capacity in the U.S.; a month later Finimize reported ASE sizing a US $2.5 bn package and test project.
  • King Yuan Electronics Company: this is another packaging and testing partner based in Taiwan. It has a small sales & customer engineering office in San Jose, CA. On March 7, 2025, the company said it is mulling whether to build a new production line in the U.S. to serve AI/HPC demand.

I remain curious to see what this tariff is going to look like for AMD, given that Tongfu Microelectronics Co may be exposed to the 100% tariff. Therefore, I'd keep an eye on the next earnings call to see management's comments. I believe there is a big chance they will reshuffle their supply chain to include only partners that are not subject to the heft tariffs on semis. That said, this is a risk worth considering.

Looking Ahead

I now see a well-positioned company with the ramp-up of the MI350 family of GPUs and strong performance in the client and even in the gaming segment. I haven't mentioned it above, but on the client side, revenue climbed 67% to $2.5 billion thanks to record desktop CPU sales and a 25% jump in commercial notebook consumption, mainly thanks to Dell. This momentum is likely to persist in the back half of the year, per the call (emphasis added):

Looking more broadly, we remain confident we can continue growing client processor revenue ahead of the market over the coming quarters, driven by increased adoption of our desktop and notebook products, growing commercial momentum and a richer product mix.

Even gaming, my least favorite segment, bounced 73% in Q2 to $1.1 billion, driven by new multiyear collaborations with Microsoft (MSFT) and Sony (SONY), combined with strong Radeon 9600 XT sell-through.

The guidance for Q3 looks quite good in my view.

At the mid-point, it represents a 28 % YOY growth excluding (and this is key) China sales. Therefore, the likely approval of the license to export the MI308 to China (I believe this quarter, as soon as the U.S. strikes a deal with China) will likely add to the optimism surrounding this stock.

At this point, and despite the sectoral tariffs risks, I cannot rate AMD anything less than a Strong Buy.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace IPO: The Space Stock Everyone Will Be Talking About
data.description:
Introduction

Just last week, I covered Figma's (FIG) IPO and said it’s arguably the most-talked-about IPO of 2025. Today, I’ll say the hottest IPO of the year might not be Figma. It might just be Firefly Aerospace Inc. (NASDAQ:FLY). It’s hard to pick between the two, because they are so different, but I know for sure that FLY is on fire. Everyone’s talking about it, and there’s a very good reason for that - it’s playing in one of the most requested industries on the market, space.

Now, I am not talking about the vague, futuristic kind of space. Firefly already has contracts, launches, and money that very important clients are ready to pay. Even Firefly itself, in its SEC filing, points out that they operate in a "highly attractive and growing industry." And they’re right. According to a 2024 McKinsey report, the global space economy is expected to hit $1.8 trillion by 2035:

We operate in a highly attractive and growing industry. According to McKinsey’s report from 2024, the global space economy is projected to reach $1.8 trillion in value by 2035 driven by accelerating national security and commercial demand. In recent years, record demand for satellites caused a supply shortage of orbital launch vehicles. In 2024, nearly 2,800 satellites launched to orbit, representing more than a 500% increase in demand for launch services compared to just five years prior, according to a 2025 report by BryceTech. The scarcity of launch capacity and increasing government and commercial demand has led these customers to seek out defense technology companies with cost-efficient and proven production systems for increasing capacity.

And this doesn’t surprise me. We’re seeing more and more investors jump into anything that has to do with space, from satellites to Moon missions, to edge computing in orbit, whatever that can be useful in the future. The fascination with space isn’t new. We (humans) have always had this dream of reaching beyond our planet. The excitement is so huge because finally, the dreams, the tech, the money, and the ambition are lining up to make it possible.

Firefly is a company that literally just landed a spacecraft on the Moon and has active contracts with NASA, the U.S. Space Force, and a list of defense agencies. As a result, the stock is expensive, but if you're trying to apply regular stock valuation rules here, you're already looking at it the wrong way. The same as AI is now hot and expensive, space is as well.

If you try to find a cheap stock in this sector, chances are you’ll end up with a company that’s cheap for a reason, either no product, no revenues, or some kind of red flag. And if it’s expensive, well, that might just mean it’s working. Look at Rocket Lab (RKLB), or at Intuitive Machines (LUNR), these are direct public competitors of Firefly. Both of those are priced for perfection, both have their own issues, but people still buy them.

So, it's not surprising that Firefly was priced at $45 per share for its IPO, but the stock soared to $70 on the opening. That’s a massive 55%. That kind of move confirms what I said earlier: this IPO is one of the most hyped of the year, and investors are ready to jump in for a pure-play space exposure, even if it's expensive.

For what it’s worth, this isn’t the first time I’ve written about the space race. If you’re curious about the bigger picture, check out my article on Amazon’s (AMZN) Project Kuiper. In that research, you will find many similar points, especially about risks and regulations, and you’ll see that it’s all connected.

About the IPO itself, most of them get a dramatic start in the beginning. We usually get a big spike on day one, and then the stock pulls back. That could very well happen here, too. But that doesn’t change the bigger story. Firefly is a very interesting stock and definitely the one to keep on your watchlist.

Near-Death to Lunar Landing

Firefly has an interesting and very special history. It was founded in 2014, but then filed for bankruptcy just three years later, and was “rescued” by Ukrainian entrepreneur Max Polyakov. But even that got complicated because the U.S. government forced Polyakov to divest due to national security concerns. So the company kept changing hands, rebuilding itself, and somehow lived to see this IPO.

Then, in March 2025, Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander touched down on the Moon, which was a massive achievement. This is what the company wrote the moment it happened:

March 2, 2025, 2:34 am CST: Successful Touchdown Confirmed

We have confirmation Blue Ghost stuck the landing! Firefly just became the first commercial company in history to achieve a fully successful Moon landing. This small step on the Moon represents a giant leap in commercial exploration.

In that moment, Firefly stopped being just another “dreaming” startup and became a real competitor of SpaceX, Rocket Lab, and Intuitive Machines. Firefly officially joined the space race.

More Than Just Moon Landing

Firefly is building a full stack of space infrastructure. They have the Alpha rocket, which is:

Firefly’s small-lift rocket, Alpha, is equipped to launch more than 1,000 kg to low Earth orbit in support of commercial, civil, and national security missions. The flight-proven vehicle is designed to be a responsive, reliable, and cost-competitive launch option, enabling direct, on-demand deliveries when and where customers need to fly.

It’s already operational for some time now. More than a year ago, there was news that Firefly Aerospace successfully launched Alpha FLTA005 Noise for NASA.

They’re working on a bigger vehicle, Elytra, which is being developed alongside Lockheed Martin and is designed to be reusable. They are getting ready for their first mission, which is a test flight. It’s scheduled for some time this year.

Firefly’s multi-mission Elytra vehicles offer in-space maneuverability and servicing when and where customers need it. Our line of highly mobile and scalable spacecraft provides on-demand payload delivery, imaging, long-haul communications, and domain awareness across cislunar space. Each configuration – Elytra Dawn, Elytra Dusk, and Elytra Dark – travels further into space to support larger payloads and more advanced services for both government and commercial customers.

Then they’ve got a lunar lander, which I already mentioned, it’s called Blue Ghost. Besides, they’re developing Ocula, which is a Lunar Imaging Service, which will help identify possible landing zones, mineral deposits on the surface of the Moon, etc.

So, besides launching rockets, they’re building vehicles, landers, satellites, and even a platform for secure data transfer in space.

Who’s Buying?

Firefly isn’t building a space tourism company, although they do have commercial ambitions (especially in satellite tech and lunar missions), but their current customer base consists of government agencies and other important organizations. For example, the U.S. government, NASA, Space Force, the Department of Defense, the NRO, and giants like Lockheed Martin and L3Harris. These are serious customers, with serious budgets.

In Q1 2025, Firefly brought in $55.9 million in revenue. That’s more than six times what they made a year ago. Their backlog is more than $1.1 billion, spread across over 30 missions. That includes launches, lunar landings, and upcoming satellite work:

Strong customer demand backs our financial profile with approximately $1.1 billion in backlog as of March 31, 2025 and multi-launch agreements across our product lines. Underpinning our financial profile is the combination of efficient contract structure and milestone-based billing. Before launch, we typically have collected approximately 90% of the total contract value, which is highly advantageous as production ramps.

It Is Expensive. But So Is Everything Else in Space

Now, the valuation is high. At $6.3 billion post-IPO, Firefly is trading at a very rich price-to-sales multiple if you look at annualized Q1 numbers. They’re losing money, $60 million in Q1 2025 alone, and their margins are still negative.

Before the IPO, they had over $170 million in cash and about $150 million in total debt.

To calculate the approximate Price to Sales ratio, I take IPO numbers first, and then I'll look at the post-IPO as well: Market Cap of $6.3 billion, and Q1 2025 revenue of $55.9 million. Let’s assume this is their new level now (they have $1.1 billion in a backlog, after all), and take the Annualized Revenue Estimate of $55.9 million × 4 = $223.6 million. P/S Ratio = Market Cap / Annualized Revenue​ = 223.6 million / 6.3 billion​ ≈ 28.2x. I assume the stock has the chance to come back to the pre-IPO valuation when the excitement calms down a little.

But until that happens, and the stock is at about $70 per share, the valuation looks much more expensive. That $6.3 billion market cap I used earlier, now got closer to $10 billion. That puts Firefly at a price-to-sales ratio of around 44x based on annualized Q1 numbers.

28 times is already a lot, not to mention 44 times. But again, that’s not crazy in this sector. Rocket Lab is trading at the same sky-high multiples - P/S of about 47x TTM. Intuitive Machines is much cheaper now, with a price-to-sales ratio of less than 4x, but LUNR has had some problems lately. When the stock was at its high, its P/S multiple was much higher than it is right now. Something like ~10x, if I am not mistaken.

This entire industry is valued based on potential, not profit. So here, you have to think about what it could become, and how far ahead of competitors it already is. I don’t think we will have the chance to buy it really cheap. LUNR level is a bargain compared to peers, but again, they had some problems, so am I personally jumping on this opportunity - not really. Although I did some nice trades with LUNR in the past. If the execution is perfect, you will be paying too much. Discount comes with issues.

Where the Money’s Going

Out of the $868 million they just raised, some will go toward cleaning up the balance sheet. They’ve got debt to pay down and preferred shares to redeem, like most young aerospace companies do. But the bigger picture here is that the majority of this money will be sponsoring future growth.

They’re already investing in expanding their rocket and lander programs, especially Blue Ghost, which already made it to the Moon and now has a second mission scheduled. They’re improving their launch infrastructure and scaling their manufacturing capabilities, because the demand is clearly coming. Besides, they've got the Ocula satellite system, which needs funding as well.

Risks

Here we have the same risk that we have for all of the other space companies: execution. We’ve seen it before, and one failed launch, one delay, one issue, and the stock can crash. This is what actually went wrong for Intuitive Machines when their lander landed on its side. The second time it happened, or in March, the stock crashed in a matter of a couple of days from $20 per share to $6. This can happen to any of these companies, so beware. On the other hand, when they have a successful launch, the stock will soar as well. So, I guess, this industry is not for the faint-hearted.

Conclusion

A 55% jump on day one is not small, and a lot of future growth is already priced in now. It means any misstep will hurt the stock, but any strong execution will still be rewarded. That’s the double-edged sword of hot IPOs in hot sectors.

So, Firefly isn’t a cheap stock, it’s not a profitable stock, and it’s definitely not a safe stock. But it’s one of the most exciting ones we have seen lately. Will it be volatile? Absolutely, the confirmation of it you can already see today, on their first trading day. I think there is a strong chance we will see a post-IPO drop. Now, what to do with this information is up to you. Someone will decide to open a position now, and DCA it down. Someone will wait and hope to catch it lower. Someone won't buy it and will call it a meme stock.

And all of them will be a little right. This is the kind of stock that will move 20% in a day on news. But if you’re looking for exposure to the space industry, and you’re willing to take on the risk that comes with that, Firefly is one of the clearest pure-play bets you can make right now. If they execute well, today’s high multiple might look a lot more reasonable a few years from now.

data.title: Natural Gas Holds Near $3.10 As Triangle Consolidation And EU Oversupply Limit Upside (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Natural gas prices are consolidating near $3.10, following a technical rebound from the $2.85–$2.95 support zone that marked a key inflection area over the past several months. The bounce, which came after a direct test of the rising trendline from September 2024, has helped stabilize sentiment in the near term.

However, broader momentum remains constrained, with the commodity continuing to compress within a symmetrical triangle formation as traders await a breakout. Overhead resistance remains dense, with the 20-, 50-, and 100-day EMAs capping rallies between $3.17 and $3.43.

Weak momentum and oversupply limit bullish conviction

Technically, the daily RSI for NG has recovered to 44.14 but remains below neutral levels, reinforcing the lack of immediate upside thrust. A move above 50 on the RSI could strengthen the case for a test of the triangle’s descending resistance near $3.45, though failure to clear this level would risk another retest of the $2.90 handle. Should price fall below $2.80, the bias would likely shift toward the $2.30 support zone, which previously contained multiple retracements in early 2024.

Fundamentally, oversupply concerns in Europe remain a key drag. Despite ongoing geopolitical tensions and the prospect of U.S. sanctions targeting Russian energy flows, European gas futures have dipped below €34/MWh. Storage buildup remains healthy, with Italy and France surpassing 79% capacity. Norwegian pipeline flows and LNG imports have added to the surplus, while reduced demand from Asia has kept interregional LNG competition muted. This dynamic continues to weigh on natural gas futures, curbing the scope for a near-term breakout despite technical support holding.

Outlook hinges on breakout resolution and storage trends

With price wedged between converging trendlines and seasonal supply factors still at play, natural gas appears poised for a larger move heading into September. A confirmed breakout above $3.45 could spark bullish momentum toward $3.80 or higher, while a breakdown below $2.85 would likely trigger a shift in sentiment and a deeper correction. For now, traders remain focused on both storage trends and geopolitical signals that may tilt the balance.

In our prior update, we noted natural gas price action was coiling within a multi-month triangle. The recent bounce confirmed the lower support band, but the lack of volume and upside momentum continues to delay a decisive move. Until that changes, traders should expect rangebound price behavior.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Central Garden & Pet Company: No Sales Growth In Sight
data.description:
Central Garden & Pet Company (NASDAQ:CENTA) manufactures and distributes products related to garden and pet supplies as the company’s name strongly suggests. CENTA owns a portfolio of good consumer staples brands in the categories, including names such as Amdro, Cadet, Four Paws, Kaytee, Nylabone, and several others.

While CENTA has had attractive long-term returns, the stock has now stagnated for several years. The company’s revenue growth has turned negative from a previously good level due to a lack of M&A and a weak organic performance, still continuing with very weakness in the fiscal Q3 report. Earnings have benefited massively from the Cost and Simplicity program, but efficiency improvements may not be enough to sustain earnings momentum for long.

CENTA’s Weak Growth Continues in Q3

CENTA’s revenue growth has stalled and even turned into an accelerating decline. After a history of constant acquisitions that bolstered the company’s wide brand portfolio, revenues first turned towards a slight decline in FY2023 and have remained on a similar path since.

The fiscal Q3 report followed the trend. CENTA’s revenues came in at $961 million, down by -4% year-on-year with a nearly similar decline in both the Pet and Garden segments. The result disappointed Wall Street’s consensus by $35 million, as CENTA doesn’t seem to be even near a sales growth turnaround.

The Pet segment’s weakness reflects CENTA’s product assortment rationalization as the company has cut lower-margin durable products and customers. On top, weak consumer confidence seems to have weighed slightly on sales as CENTA relates to soft demand, hitting durables especially hard as CENTA’s Pet portfolio is concentrating increasingly on consumables. At the same time, the Garden segment was pressured by cool and rainy weather during Q3, on top of which CENTA lost two product lines in the segment’s distribution business. Altogether, there seemed to be many factors still impacting sales strength in Q3.

Positively, CENTA still notes having maintained market share in the Pet segment, and even noted market share gains in several product categories in the Garden segment; recent revenue weakness mostly reflects an industry-wide downturn. As comparisons, The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM) reported a -1% organic revenue decline in the latest report, and Spectrum Brands Holdings, Inc. (SPB) reported a very steep -11.1% organic sales decline amid slowing shipments and slow demand – the operating environment clearly hasn’t been easy. Both of the mentioned major competitors have also reported very slow growth in the past few years.

Ultimately, it still doesn’t seem that CENTA’s sales performance is improving anytime soon. The company’s long-term target is only set at low single-digit revenue growth, but even achieving such growth appears to be challenging as sales continue to decline. CENTA still relates to a poor Q4 outlook amid consumer confidence pressure.

Rising private label penetration poses a risk to CENTA’s branded product portfolio, and while CENTA also manufactures private label products for retailers, the company won’t capture as great margins from the category. CENTA notes increasing humanization of pets as a long-term industry tailwind, but at the same time, the share of pet-owning households has declined from 54% in 2019 to 51% in 2023.

Cost and Simplicity Program Continues to Improve Earnings For Now

CENTA still has a lot of positives to note in the earnings performance – the Q3 adjusted EPS reached $1.56, growing by $0.24 year-on-year and beating Wall Street’s consensus by $0.13. Adjusted operating income came in at $135 million, up by 9% through a 170 basis point margin improvement.

Behind the earnings increase, CENTA’s multi-year Cost and Simplicity program has driven significant gross margin gains and offset negative operating leverage in operating expenses. CENTA’s product assortment rationalization has improved the product margin mix, and manufacturing consolidation has improved efficiency on top of other initiatives. Representatively, the gross margin showed a 280 basis point improvement to 34.6% in the latest report.

The productivity program has already driven CENTA’s operating margin to a new long-term record level – CENTA’s weak sales are partially a fair compromise as the company has clearly optimized the product portfolio performance to a much better performance. A history of M&A previously made the organization too complex, and the program is still ongoing and seems to be driving further margin gains in the future as well. Yet, over the long term, I believe that CENTA’s sales performance will be more critical for the earnings outlook as declining sales need to be met with constant cost cuts to salvage margins – SG&A increased by 30 basis points as a percentage of sales in Q3, driving negative operating leverage as sales shrink.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model to estimate a fair value for CENTA. After macroeconomic weakness subsides and CENTA ends its product assortment rationalization, I estimate sales growth to rebound to a slow 1% perpetual level. The estimate suggests growth slightly below average inflation.

While CENTA’s margins are improving, I ultimately assume the margin will moderate due to weak sales growth. I expect the EBIT margin to stabilize at a fairly good 8% level. CENTA’s after-tax cash flow conversion should be very good as the company doesn’t have a significant need for capital expenditures.

The estimates put CENTA’s fair value at $38.11, 15% above the CENTA listing’s price at the time of writing. The valuation currently provides a moderate margin of safety, but for good reason as there currently isn’t clarity for a sales growth turnaround.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.49%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 4.82% interest rate, annualized from CENTA’s Q2 interest expenses and debt. I assume a fairly high 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.25% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the U.S. I use Aswath Damodaran’s 0.90 beta estimate for the household products industry. With a liquidity premium of 0.25%, the cost of equity stands at 8.66% and the WACC at 7.49%.

Takeaway

CENTA’s Q3 report still showed very weak sales without visibility for a growth turnaround; macroeconomic pressure and low general category growth are still weighing on the company. Yet, the Cost and Simplicity program is driving significant margin improvements, pushing up earnings despite weak sales. The longer-term outlook is still more dependent on a return to sales growth, though – efficiency can’t be improved forever. The stock is valued with a fair margin of safety, and as such, I initiate CENTA at a Hold rating.
